coredata.dc:title: Comprehensive spectroscopic studies of synergism between Gadong starch based carbon dots and bovine serum albumin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Sonthanasamy, Regina Sisika A.
Sulaiman, Nik Muslihuddin Nik
Tan, Ling Ling
Lazim, Azwan Mat
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots) were used to study the binding mechanisms with serum protein, bovine serum albumin (BSA) by using two notable binding systems known as non-covalent and covalent interaction. Interaction between C-dots and BSA were estimated by Stern-Volmer equation and Double Log Regression Model (DLRM). According to the fluorescent intensity, quenching of model carrier protein by C-dots was due to dynamic quenching for non-covalent and static quenching for covalent binding. The binding site constant, KA and number of binding site, for covalent interaction is 1754.7L/mol and n≈1 (0.6922) were determined by DLRM on fluorescence quenching results. The blue shift of the fluorescence spectrum, from 450nm to 421nm (non-covalent) and 430nm (covalent) and suggested that both the microenvironment of C-dots and protein changed in relation to the protein concentration. The fluorescence intensity results show that protein structure has a significant role in Protein-C-dots interactions and type of binding influence physicochemical properties of C-dots differently. Understanding to this bio interface is important to utilize both quantum dots and biomolecules for biomedical field. It can be a useful guideline to design further applications in biomedical and bioimaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: C-dots
Quantum dots
Binding interaction
Bovine serum albumin
Fluorescence quenching

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Immobilization of carbon dots with protein
Physical interaction of C-dots and protein
Morphologies and surface properties of C-dots
UV–vis spectrum analysis of protein immobilization with C-dots
Bonding configurations of C-dots and protein
Morphology of immobilized C-dots with protein
Binding isotherms of C-dots with protein

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterisations of C-dots by Transmission electron microscope (a), UV–Vis spectroscopy (b), Raman spectroscopy (c), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (d-f) and PL decay for C-dots correspond to life time (g).
(a) & (c) Non-covalent and (b) & (d) covalent interaction of C-dots with BSA at different concentrations and their life-span.
UV–vis spectrum of (a) Non-covalent and (b) covalent interaction of C-dots with BSA.
FTIR spectrum of C-dots with BSA.
TEM image of immobilization of C-dots with protein by non-covalent (a) and covalent interaction.
Height (left) (ai)(bi) and SenseHeight (right) (aiii)(bii) images from the AFM analysis of the C-dots-BSA conjugate sample on mica substrate.
Stern–Volmer plot of fluorescence quenching of C-dots with different concentration of BSA protein.
Stern–Volmer plot of quenching constant of C-dots with different concentration of BSA protein.
Plot of double log regression model of C-dots-BSA complex.

(a) The structure of BSA and the simulation result showing the expected location of the four binding sites (1–4) for C-dots and (b) Theoretical interaction of proteins to C-dots using EDC and sulfo-NHS [26,27].
Optimisation of BSA functionalisation on C-dots.
The displacement of emission peak of C-dots-BSA complex.
Summary of Stern–Volmer equation analysis for protein immobilization.
Stern–Volmer equation analysis for various protein concentrations.
Binding constant of BSA protein binding with C-dots.

head.ce:title: Comprehensive spectroscopic studies of synergism between Gadong starch based carbon dots and bovine serum albumin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences and Food Technology, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Sciences and Food Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Southest Asia Disaster Preventation Research Initiative (SEADPRI-UKM), Institute for Environment and Development (LESTARI), Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Southest Asia Disaster Preventation Research Initiative (SEADPRI-UKM)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (C-dots) were used to study the binding mechanisms with serum protein, bovine serum albumin (BSA) by using two notable binding systems known as non-covalent and covalent interaction. Interaction between C-dots and BSA were estimated by Stern-Volmer equation and Double Log Regression Model (DLRM). According to the fluorescent intensity, quenching of model carrier protein by C-dots was due to dynamic quenching for non-covalent and static quenching for covalent binding. The binding site constant, KA and number of binding site, for covalent interaction is 1754.7L/mol and n≈1 (0.6922) were determined by DLRM on fluorescence quenching results. The blue shift of the fluorescence spectrum, from 450nm to 421nm (non-covalent) and 430nm (covalent) and suggested that both the microenvironment of C-dots and protein changed in relation to the protein concentration. The fluorescence intensity results show that protein structure has a significant role in Protein-C-dots interactions and type of binding influence physicochemical properties of C-dots differently. Understanding to this bio interface is important to utilize both quantum dots and biomolecules for biomedical field. It can be a useful guideline to design further applications in biomedical and bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon is an interesting element in nature and their existence is found in different forms and names such as soot, charcoal, graphite and diamonds. This fourth most abundant element in the universe has been applied in many areas, from petrochemicals, lubricants, material, biomedicals, sensors and many more. The transformation of carbon intro high-tech material is tremendously interesting especially as material for sensing as example various analytes or cells imaging owing to their tenacious photoluminescence biocompatibility as well as photo and chemical stability.

Carbon based quantum dots (CDs) also has been discovered as nontoxic fluorescent nanoparticles with high biocompatibility to replace fluorescence semiconductor nanocrystal such as CdTe, CdSe, CdS due to their biotoxicity of the heavy metal especially cadmium, the main unit of semiconductor nanocrystals [1]. Moreover, these well-known carbon based nanomaterials namely as carbon nanotube, carbon nanohorn, graphite, nanodiamond and carbon dots (C-dots) have established their applications in nanotechnology, biosensing and drug delivery [2–7].

Usually, surface modification of carbon nanomaterials is necessary for biological applications. Nanoparticles have a greater surface area which makes them to interact with numerous organic moieties and biomolecules including drugs, enzyme, vitamins and proteins. Mostly the surface of C-dots was modified with polycations such as Branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI), diamine-terminated oligomeric poly (ethylene glycol) (PEG1500N), poly(propionylethylenimine-co-ethylenimine) (PPEI-EI), and polyethyleneimine (PEI) [8]. This surface passivation is to enhance the solubility, surface energy stabilization, strong photoluminescence, stable towards photobleaching and high quantum yield [9–12]. C-dots properties turns into an excellent performance when the surface is modified with cations by increasing fluorescent intensity, maintaining the luminescent property in water for a long period and reducing the toxicity effect [9,10,13–15]. Besides polycations, C-dots conjugation with biomolecules is important to enhance the C-dots properties in biomedical field. Recently, protein conjugated C-dots has become a common candidate in biomedical field. This is due to the serum proteins which are known as a crucial transport protein in Homo sapiens. Advantage of transport proteins is they able to bind with both endogenous and exogenous drugs excessively [16,17]. This type of protein is being used in drug delivery system due to their selectivity towards targeted disease. Therefore, the conjugation between C-dots and serum protein is encourageable because protein itself has their own luminescent property which used to track the changes of protein during binding [18–20].

Various binding methods have been applied between biomolecules and carbon based quantum dots [21–27] to make them to be an excellent vehicle as a drug, genes carrier and to be used for imaging in biology as well as in medicine. As an example, Cheng et al. [28] were did the binding studies between tetrandrine and bovine serum albumin/human serum albumin was performed using fluorescence titration method in the presence of three site markers. Besides that, a method using Field effect transistors (FETs) device with polymer-coated carbon nanotube (CNTs) was developed to study protein binding by Star et al. [29]. Furthermore, amide activation compound like 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDAC) was used as an intermediate to form binding interaction between carbon nanotubes and BSA under room temperature by Huang et al. [30]. On the other hand, many analytical techniques, such as atomic force microscopy, circular dichroism spectroscopy and fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (FCS) have been used to study the interaction between quantum dots and biomolecules [31–37]. In addition, detail studies of the kinetics and thermodynamic characteristic of biomolecule interactions was examine using FCS technique [39–42] So far, FCS method is the most common technique has been applied to investigate various biomolecules [43–47].

Potential biological applications of C-dots have received strong attention. Some recent studies regarding C-dots have been focus on their capability for biological field and introduce C-dots into biological systems. For example, surface functionalized C-dots with appropriate biomolecules make them a potential biosensor for in vitro bioimaging [48]. Besides, C-dots was used as a biosensor carrier due to their biocompatibility, photostability, soluble in water and flexible surface for modification [49–55]. Based On previous investigations, C-dots are excellent candidate for immobilization of biomolecules compared to organic dyes [56–58]. Therefore the interaction between C-dots and biomolecules is important to be investigated before it been widely use in life science.

Biomolecule of protein are play an important role in biological function and studies on their function and interactions are vital for life science. Proteins play dual-functional ability which is transporting various biological molecules in blood, meanwhile maintaining normal osmotic pressure of blood [59]. One of the essential aspect serum protein was it has wide application in pharmacological and biomedical research. Among them bovine serum albumin (BSA) are important high serum proteins. Thus, this protein molecule is selected to investigate further in this research.

Transport protein especially serum protein have special advantage such as low toxicity [60], high solubility, biodegradability and biocompatibility [61,62], and cheap [63]. They could be utilized as a therapeutic molecule carrier into cells since they are easily enter into tumor cells [64]. Generally, BSA is an acidic pH environment which is the main factor to be a favorable molecule for the microenvironment of tumors [65,66]. Bioconjugation of BSA with C-dots has dual function which is known as a linker and also enhances the fluorescence property of C-dots. The immobilization of various proteins on C-dots can perform by two binding interaction known as noncovalent (e.g., adsorption) and covalent conjugation. Covalent attachment of proteins on C-dots using cross-linker 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide (EDC) was first reported by Huang et al. [30] and Jiang et al. [67]. Since then, it has been widely used in the covalent immobilization of proteins on C-dots. Few researches criticize the covalent interaction between protein is lacking and mostly the interaction is due to adsorption [27].

Fluorescence properties of C-dots is a main concern for many biologically applications. Surface modification of C-dots with biomolecules such as proteins causes an effect on fluorescent property. Therefore the interaction between C-dots and biomolecules is important to be studied because biomolecules such as protein act as a supporting agent to carry drugs, antibodies, metals and many other molecules into biological system. So far, studies on the interaction of C-dots and BSA have been reported very less. The investigation gives information regarding interaction mechanism between C-dots and BSA by providing an important reference for the analysis of the pharmacodynamics mechanism of C-dots. Hera, we report the functionalization of C-dots with bovine serum albumin (BSA) proteins via non covalent and covalent method. In this paper, starch nanoparticles from Gadong tuber was used as a precursor material to synthesis C-dots. The morphology and interaction between C-dots and protein was investigated.

All chemicals were of reagent grade and used without further purification. The reagent grade of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide, (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide, (NHS) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Anhydrous acetone was purchased from ACROS, USA. Ultrapure water (~18.2MΩ.cm, 25°C) was obtained from a water purifying system (Sartorius, Model Arium®611DI). Gadong starch nanoparticles prepared according to the method reported by Kim et al. [68].

Previously synthesized carbon dots (C-dots) from Gadong starch nanocrystal was used in this research [69]. The starch nanoparticles were then converted to carbon nanoparticles by dehydration with concentrated sulfuric acid method based on Chin's et al. [70]. In typical synthesis, 2.0g of starch nanoparticles were dispersed in 5mL of ultrapure water and 8.0mL of concentrated sulfuric acid was added to the starch solution. The reaction was allowed to proceed for 40min, followed by the additional of 40mL of ultra-pure water. The black carbon nanoparticles produced was collected by centrifugation (20,000 g, 20min) and washed three times with ultra-pure water. The black carbon nanoparticles were dispersed in 20mL of nitric acid (2.0M) and the mixture was refluxed for 1h. The solution was neutralised by sodium hydroxide at room temperature. The C-dots particles were collected using acetone and then dried, before the solid weight of the C-dots was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of carbon dots with protein

ce:section.ce:para: Protein interaction studies with C-dots were carried out according to the method reported by Mewada et al. [71]. Aqueous stock solution (3000ppm) of BSA was prepared by dissolving 30mg of BSA in 10mL of ultra-pure water. C-dots were used at a concentration of 2mg/mL for the attachment studies. The concentration of BSA was tried for functionalization of C-dots is shown in Table 1 and prepared solutions were incubated at room temperature for 2h.

Covalent interaction between protein and C-dots was conducted based on the method Jiang et al. [67]. About 5.0mg/mL of C-dots were suspended in ultra-pure water by sonicating the mixture for a short period of time. Then, 1.0mL of a 500mM MES buffer (2-morpholinoethanesulfonic acid) solution (pH=6.1) and 2.3mL of a 50mg/mL NHS aqueous solution were added to the above suspension and mixed. Under fast stirring, 1.2mL fresh EDC aqueous solution (10mg/mL) was added quickly, and the mixture was continually stirred at room temperature for 30min. The suspension was then filtered through a 0.45mm hydrophilized PTFE membrane. 1.0mL of a 10mg/mL BSA in MES buffer solution (pH=6.1) was added to the estered C-dots. The mixture was shooked on a platform shaker at 150rpm at room temperature for 1h. The protein–C-dots conjugates were kept in 4°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical interaction of C-dots and protein
ce:section.ce:para: Interaction of the C-dots and protein were studied by fluorescence spectrophotometry at room temperature. The interactions between C-dots and increasing concentrations of Bovine Serum Albumin (BSA) were studied in an aqueous solution. The fluorescence spectra were collected with excitation at 340nm of C-dots-protein were recorded between 340nm to 800nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphologies and surface properties of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Acid dehydration followed by surface oxidation was conducted on starch nanocrystal to obtain C-dots and analyzed under transmission electron microscope (TEM). The obtained C-dots are spherical in shape with size diameter from 6 to 25nm. Basically, the size and shape of C-dots is depends on the starting material and the technique used to synthesis C-dots. Previous researches have reported that smaller sized C-dots (<5nm) usually produced through the pyrolysis of natural products such as green tea, coffee, eggshell and potato [72–77]. The combustion soot of a natural gas burner produced C-dots in range of 4.8±0.6nm in size [78]. C-dots exhibited an absorbance peak at 296nm. The absorption is typically due to the presence of n→π⁎ electron transitions associated with the CO functional group on the surface of C-dots.

Raman spectroscopy is highly sensitive towards compounds that contain carbon. Therefore, Vibrational properties of C-dots were analyzed by Raman spectroscopy. Individual Raman spectra of the sample are shown in Fig. 1 . According to the spectra, C-dots contain characteristic D and G bands at 1350 and 1600cm−1, respectively. The intensity of the D-band shows the amount of disordered sp3 carbon and the intensity of G-band shows the amount of ordered graphitic (sp2) carbon contained in the C-dots. The synthesized C-dots contain more ordered graphitic (sp2) carbon. The graphitization degree of the synthesized C-dots is 0.87° (ID/IG) [79]. Low graphitization degree would lead to a good electrical conductivity [79].

Functional groups and elemental composition on the surface of C-dots was determined through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. The C1s spectra of the C-dots through chemical method shows three types of carbon bonds such as CC or CH (284.7eV), CO or CC (286.1eV), and CO (288.2eV). Besides, the O1s spectra of the C-dots shows two types of oxygen bonds such as CO (533.0eV) and CO (532.0eV). The C:O weight ratio of C-dots depends on the elemental composition of the starting material which used to affect the composition of C-dots. Though C-dots prepared from carbohydrates such as sweet potatoes had hydroxyl and carboxylic/carbonyl groups on their surfaces, their C:O weight ratios were relatively lower than those synthesized from other natural products [75,80,81].

The decays in the luminescence lifetime of the C-dots are very well fitted to tri-exponential function. The decay parameters, as obtained from fitting the raw decay data are given in Table 2 . Lifetime of C-dots was calculated using Eq. (1) as follows:
(1)
τ avg
=
B 1
τ 1 2
+
B 2
τ 2 2
+
B 3
τ 3 2
B 1
τ 1
+
B 2
τ 2
+
B 3
τ 3
where (τavg) is the average lifetime of C-dots, Bx (x=1,2,3) are the pre-exponential factors, τx (x=1,2,3) are the decay times. Decay spectrum shown in Fig. 1 (g), indicating the observed lifetime (τavg) is 2.1ns with a correction, χ2 of 1.014. The lifetime is much longer than that of carbonaceous dots (τ=0.5ns).

Fig. 2 shows the fluorescence quenching spectra of C-dots-BSA complex at different concentration. From the non-covalent conjugation (Fig. 2a), it can be seen that at an excitation wavelength of 340nm, the maximum emission wavelength is 450nm for C-dots and the fluorescence intensity of C-dots increased with increasing concentration of BSA. Whereas for the covalent interaction (Fig. 2b), the fluorescence intensity of C-dots decreased with increasing concentration of BSA. A blue-shift in the maximum emission wavelength of fluorescence was observed for both the non-covalent and covalent interaction, as summarized in Table 3 . The steady increase in the fluorescence C-dots after non-covalent interaction with BSA strongly indicate that some conformational changes are occurring due to the interaction suggesting availability of more binding sites for C-dots [82]. Fluorescence quenching can generally be classified as either static or dynamic and static and dynamic quenching may also occur simultaneously. Due to the fluorescence quenching, binding can be analyzed through the fluorescence intensity or the shifting of the emission wavelength of the C-dots upon addition of the protein. The steady increase in fluorescent intensity is due to the dynamic quenching which results from excited fluorophore states [86]. This means the binding sites of C-dots are more prevalent on the protein moiety [19]. The blue shift of fluorescence spectrum indicate that the polarity decreases and the hydrophobicity increases for the protein molecule [86].

The decreased in the fluorescence C-dots after covalent interaction with BSA indicated that the interaction between C-dots and BSA was happened successfully and causes alteration in the microenvironment of the protein. Some conformational changes are occurring due to the interaction suggesting availability of more binding sites for C-dots. Therefore, result obtained suggested that the both binding interaction increases the hydrophobic microenvironment of protein [83].

The decays in the luminescence lifetime of the C-dots-BSA by non-covalent and covalent are very well fitted to tri-exponential function. The decay parameters, as obtained from fitting the raw decay data are given in Table 4 . Lifetime of C-dots-BSA was calculated using Eq. (1) as above. Decay spectrum of C-dots-BSA is shown in Fig. 2(c) & (d), indicating the observed lifetime (τavg) of both interaction is 2.2ns with a correction, χ2 of 1.007 and 1.040. No significant different was observed in the lifetime of C-dots which was interacted with BSA. Both type of interaction between C-dots and BSA doesn't give a significant impact on the lifetime of C-dots. The fluorescence properties of C-dots remain stable after form chemical interaction between BSA. These finding is comply with the luminescence lifetimes are independent of chemical and physical factor which include but not limited to probe concentration, inhomogeneity and geometry, as well as the intensity of the excitation light and photobleaching [87,88].

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis spectrum analysis of protein immobilization with C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: C-dots can interact with proteins with two binding mechanisms. C-dots either interact with protein by covalent or by adsorption binding mechanisms. Fig. 3 shows the absorption spectrum of interaction between C-dots with BSA by non-covalent (a) and covalent (b) binding mechanism. C-dots exhibited an absorbance peak at 296nm. The absorption peak of the serum protein occurred at 280nm, which is due to the amino acid with aromatic rings in BSA. Functionalization of C-dots with BSA was happened by breaking of the carbonyl bond and formation of amide bond in the presence of EDC and NHS. Both C-dots and BSA were interact by chemical binding which is call as covalent bond. It can be seen in Fig. 3 (b), where the carbonyl bond at 296nm was broken.

Besides that, proteins can interact with C-dots without any breaking and formation of new chemical bonds. This is call as an adsorption binding method. C-dots can adsorb onto the BSA. The carbonyl bond of C-dots was still remained but a minor red shift of 1nm and broad absorption peak was observed (296nm to 297nm) and these is likely due to the accumulation of C-dots on BSA happened [71]. Some weak interaction between functional group of BSA and C-dots might cause minor changes in the UV spectra [71]. Therefore, the interaction between C-dots and protein happened without breaking and forming new bonds but it happened by the presence of disulphide (SS) linkage in BSA which holds the C-dots [71].

Both the binding mechanism are shown in Scheme 1 [26,27]. Based on the structural analysis, BSA protein have four interaction sites (as shown in Scheme 1a). The first interaction site 1 resides is in the central cavity of the BSA which gives good binding interaction between other molecules. Sites 2–4 are situated at the interface of two subdomains in the neighborhood (Site 2 resides at the interface between subdomains IA and IB. Site 3 resides at the interface between subdomains IIA and IIB, and site 4 resides at the interface between subdomains IIIA and IIIB) which contain substitutions of amino acids that create interactions across inter-domain boundaries within the BSA. There was a carboxyl groups on the C-dots with specific positively-charged amino acid residues which attributed to the high affinity by both electrostatic reaction and hydrogen-bonding interactions. According to scheme 1 (b) covalent interaction of proteins to C-dots using a two-step procedure. The very first step is using EDC, where the unstable amide bond formed between carboxylate group and EDC. Second step was stabilizing the unstable amide bond by using sulfo-NHS and furthered with protein attachment, which formed stable amide bond.

Fluorescence particles or probe play an important role in biomedical field. Therefore, studies towards the interaction between fluorescence particles and other molecules or ions or ligands such as protein or drug are vital in understanding the mechanisms. Fluorescence serves as a sensitive method indicating alterations of the fluorophore environment. Many molecules, ligands, drugs and biomaterials are not fluorescent but proteins have a fluorescent property. This fluorescent characteristics allows the monitoring of alterations or structural changes during binding. Due to fluorescence quenching, binding can be analyzed through reduction of fluorescence intensity or the shifting of the emission wavelength of the fluorescence properties of protein or particles upon addition of foreign molecules. As an example, fluorescent denatured bovine serum albumin (dBSA)-protected gold nanocluster (AuNCs) was used to analysis in detecting acetylcholinesterase (AChE) activity in human serum. Gold nanocluster was used as a label-free fluorescent sensor due to their fluorescent property. The successful interaction between AuNCs and dBSA able to detect AChE with the increase in enzyme activity of the AChE, the fluorescence of the AuNCs was decreased [89]. Besides that, cationic fluorescent nanoparticles which shows efficient gene delivery by conjugating with green fluorescent protein plasmid or red fluorescence protein plasmid in real time intracellular fluorescence imaging. The efficient gene expression demonstrated cationic fluorescent nanoparticles is a promising non-viral platform for therapeutic nuclei acids delivery [90]. Moreover, fluorescent BSA matrix was used as a pH probe in anticancer drug studies. The fluorescent BSA nanosensor shows a fast response of intracellular pH changes in HeLa cells, whereby a significant change in fluorescence ratio was observed when the pH of the cell was switched from normal to acidic with anticancer drug treatment [91].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bonding configurations of C-dots and protein
ce:section.ce:para: The Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectrum obtained for C-dots and surface functionalised C-dots with BSA are shown in Fig. 4 . For the C-dots, peak at 3369cm−1 assigned to the OH stretching vibration. The second band is at 1640cm−1 assigned to the CO stretching vibration means that some carbonyl compounds are present in these sample. Finally, the additional peak can be seen at 1391cm−1 which corresponding to the aliphatic CH3 bond and 1095cm−1 for CO bond. Besides that, the FTIR peak of pure BSA at 3283, 2957, 1645, and 1531cm−1 are assigned to the stretching vibration of OH, amide (mainly –NH stretching vibration), amide I(mainly CO stretching vibrations), and amide II (the coupling of bending vibrate of NH and stretching vibrate of CN) bands, respectively.

Subsequently, for non-covalent interactions it indicates that the peak absorption at 3330cm−1 is due to the OH stretching of C-dots and BSA. The bands at 2932 and 2863cm−1 became faded due to the accumulation of dot-K on amino acids at OH stretching of carboxylic acid and NH amide stretching. The shift of band at 1637cm−1 (1645cm−1 and 1531cm−1 from BSA and 1640cm−1 from dot-K) was due to a reaction involving the formation of aromatic CC, amide 1° NH and anhydride CO stretching between amino acids and dot-K moieties. The reduction in the intensity of the band at 1100cm−1 (with reference to BSA at 1084cm−1) was due to the surface of protein was covered with dot-K on various sides through weak electrostatic interactions [71].

For covalent bonding interaction, as shown in Fig. 4, the spectrum of dot-K functionalised with BSA contains the absorption band of CH2 at 2943cm−1 and CN at 1304cm−1. The peak at 1591cm−1 is caused by stretching of CC. Furthermore, the sharp peak at 1650cm−1 shows an amide bond (CONH) and it suggests that BSA has been bonded to the dot-K surface by amide bonds. In addition, the absorption peak at 3311cm−1 was due to the OH stretching of dot-K. Loss of bands at 2932 and 2863cm−1 may cause by accumulation of dot-K on amino acids at OH stretching of carboxylic acid and NH amide stretching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology of immobilized C-dots with protein
ce:section.ce:para: TEM image of Fig. 5 shows non-covalent immobilization which is the adsorption of C-dots onto the surface of BSA and covalent immobilization between C-dots with BSA. For the non-covalent binding, electrostatic interaction and hydrogen bonding causes the adsorption to be happened onto the surface of BSA and an agglomerate image was shown with biggest size particles, 134nm. Comparing both interaction methods, covalent immobilization shows better morphology with smallest size particles, 30–44nm. Although covalent interaction shows better morphology and smallest size particles but assume the adsorption it were still happened at certain site of BSA.

The interaction between protein and BSA was observed by AFM. The C-dots-BSA conjugate samples were deposited on mica substrate for atomic force microscopy (AFM) analyses. Fig. 6 shows the Height (left) (ai)(bi) and SenseHeight (right) (aiii)(bii) images from the AFM analysis of the C-dots-BSA conjugate sample on mica substrate. As shown in Fig. 6 (a) and (b), the BSA protein are closely linked with C-dots by two way conjugation. The AFM characterization suggests that the C-dots were self-assembled onto the surface of BSA as shown in Fig. 6a (i,ii,iii). For the covalent interaction, as shown in Fig. 6(b), a cyclic form and well-arranged structure and accumulation of particles at certain site can be observed. Therefore, it can be concluded although covalent interaction provide smallest size particles and well-structured particles but the interaction between protein and C-dots always in two forms which is covalent and certain C-dots particles can be absorbed onto the surface of protein due to the electrostatic interaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding isotherms of C-dots with protein

ce:section.ce:para:
The changes in interaction between C-dots and protein reflect in the change of the fluorescence intensity of C-dots. These changes in the fluorescence intensity of protein analyzed by the Stern-Volmer equation and the trends of binding affinity of C-dots and protein can be understood. The observed fluorescence quenching probably arise from the energy transfer occurring between C-dots and protein. Generally, quenching mechanisms classified into dynamic quenching and static quenching. The dynamic quenching conforms to the Stern-Volmer equation. The fluorescence quenching mechanism can be analyzed quantitatively at different concentrations of proteins at constant temperature with the Stern– Volmer equation:
(2)
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K q
τ 0
Q
= 1 +
K SV
Q

F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of the C-dots in the absence and presence of added quencher, respectively; [Q] is the concentration of the quencher; Kq is the rate constant of dimolecular quenching; τ0 is the average lifetime of the C-dots without quencher, mol/L; Ksv is the quenching constant of Stern-Volmer, L/mol (Kq=KSV/τ0). Stern-Volmer plots of the fluorescence quenching between C-dots and protein are shown in Fig. 7 and the calculated quenching constants are given in Table 5 . If the increasing of concentration of protein gives negative effects to the Kq, then the quenching is static quenching; if the increasing of concentration of protein gives positive effects, then the quenching is dynamic. The detail calculation of each concentration of protein for both binding interaction are shown in Table 6 and the trend of both binding method are shown in Fig. 8 . It was found that the value of Kq increased with increasing concentration of protein, which proved that the quenching between C-dots and protein is dynamic for non-covalent interactions whereas for the covalent interaction, the value of Kq dropped with increasing concentration of protein shows that the quenching between C-dots and protein is static.

In case of non-covalent interaction between C-dots and BSA, there was a gradual increase in Kq up to the concentration of 150ppm of BSA then it suddenly dropped slightly at 200ppm of BSA. The changes in Kq is due to conformational changes are occurred in BSA which are also seen in the fluorescence spectra of C-dots-BSA. Therefore, at certain range of BSA concentration (from 5 to 150ppm), there is high chance for conformational changes to occur and increase fluorescence due to structural rigidity steric hindrance may occur against acquiring electrostatic interaction with C-dots because of high affinity towards them [82].

As shown in Table 6, a slight decrease in fluorescence quenching (Kq) up to 15ppm and a sudden increases at 20ppm and then subsequently decreased up to 200ppm for covalent interaction between C-dots and protein. The increase in Kq indicates more binding affinity of C-dots to BSA and its susceptibility to the conformational changes up to its certain concentration (20ppm). The further increase in the concentration of protein reduces the binding affinity for C-dots. This may be because of steric hindrance for BSA at higher concentration which may be either because of saturation of C-dots surface or loss of co-operative binding [82].

The binding constant (KA) between C-dots and quenching molecules can be calculated by Eq. (3) [84,85]:
(3)
Log
F 0
– F
/ F
= Log
K A
+ n Log
Q

F0 and F are fluorescence intensities of C-dots in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively; [Q] is the concentration of the quencher; KA is the binding constant, L/mol; n is the number of binding sites. Combining the experimental data with Eq. (3) by drawing double logarithmic plots of Log[Q] and Log [(F0 –F)/F] for each binding reaction system, resulted in plots as shown in Fig. 9 . The calculated results including the binding constants between C-dots and protein, BSA are summarized in Table 7 . Table 6 shows; (1) The molecule binding constants of C-dots and serum protein by covalent binding are higher than non-covalent binding, and there is approximately one binding site. (2) The binding differences of the C-dots with protein molecules are given impacts on the binding constant, which indicated that C-dots and BSA formed by covalent interaction is relatively stable complexes and its assumed some of the C-dots was absorbed certain site of BSA which indirectly influence the stability of the complex.

In summary, serum protein, BSA which belong to globular protein family interact differently with carbon quantum dots. For both the binding interaction, a general trend was observed at protein concentrations from 5ppm to 200ppm, quenching constant, KSV to C-dots is directly proportional to rate constant, Kq. Both the binding mechanisms have different electrostatic interactions with C-dots reflecting in different quenching and rate constants. Moreover, the concentration of the protein also has the influence on the protein-C-dots interactions. According to fluorescence spectra, Stern–Volmer equation, and double log regression model, BSA is structurally unstable after interaction with C-dots by non-covalent and covalent interaction. In case of C-dots-BSA by non-covalent interaction, the increase in quenching suggests conformational change in three dimensional structure of the protein in such way that more binding sites became available for interaction with C-dots resulting in the increase in fluorescence quenching. Similarly, in case of covalent interaction, BSA which consists of alpha helices as a major constituent in three-dimensional structure of protein, the conformation may change at a particular concentration and thereafter a significant drop in fluorescence quenching was observed. In conclusion, this study reflects that nanoparticle-protein interactions depend not only on the surface chemistry of the nanoparticle but also on the protein structure. Globular proteins interact differently and have variable effects on the fluorescence property of the carbon dots. Therefore, c-dots can adsorb onto the BSA surface or can covalently interact with BSA. However, covalent interaction is more stable than the non-covalent interaction. The understanding of these interactions is important in the field of biomedical and bioengineering research and advanced.
